item.text: The four directional actions consistently translate the large colour-9 cross by three pixels, so that is the controlled avatar. `USE` is contextual: at the initial cross position it changes the cross’s centre and removes one colour-11 cell elsewhere, which points to an interaction/push mechanic rather than movement. I’m now compacting the geometry into row runs and testing short repeated-action contexts.

The geometry now gives a concrete dense objective: each colour’s four yellow-ring centres lie on a single cross. Colour 9’s targets intersect at `(24,48)` and colour 11’s at `(9,15)`; both are reachable on the three-pixel movement lattice. I’m validating the full two-cross alignment on a clone before committing it to the leg library.

item.text: The clone reaches reward exactly on move 20, when the second cross completes its alignment. I’m packaging this as a frame-derived reusable leg: it detects ring centres, infers each cross’s current and target intersection, identifies the active cross from its hollow centre, and performs only the required lattice moves and switch. The level player will contain just the leg call.

Implemented level 1 using a reusable, frame-derived cross-alignment leg.

- [legs.py](/Users/sasha/gkm/arc/crack_lab/runs/scratch/gkm_legs_ws_re86_cheap_high_screen/legs.py): detects rings, cross axes, active cross, and movement lattice.
